fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need a small notification dispatch library for our CI pipelines. Right now every job is basically copy-pasting the same messy logic to push messages into our chat workspace and it's causing all sorts of broken messages — especially when branch names or PR titles have weird characters in them. Someone's PR title had angle brackets last week and completely blew up the channel message lol.

The library should handle rendering text safely for the chat workspace (similar to what we did in the old Jenkins integration, you can check that codebase for reference), putting together a standard 'build started' line, and actually sending the message to one or more channels without the job having to know anything about HTTP or auth tokens.

On the delivery side, people sometimes want to blast the same notification to a whole list of rooms at once — the channel field might have multiple targets in it separated by different delimiters, so we need to handle that gracefully. We also have two different ways we send messages depending on the credential type (the webhook path vs the newer bot account method), and those need to route to totally different upstream URLs with different query parameters.

Also there's a custom message feature where users can override the notification text per build outcome — we just need a quick utility to check whether any of those slots have actually been filled in before we bother appending them.

Please keep the code clean and modular, not one giant file.

trigger_keywords: how are HTML anchor tags handled
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: An HTML anchor of the form `LABEL` is collapsed into the workspace's inline-link syntax ``. The surrounding quote characters on the URL are preserved exactly as written (single or double quotes stay in the output). This conversion happens BEFORE the reserved-character encoding step.

trigger_keywords: reserved characters in the chat protocol
what does the escaper do to ampersands and angle brackets
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Exactly three characters are entity-encoded: `&` → `&`, `<` → `<`, `>` → `>`. All other characters — including `%`, `{`, `}`, `#`, and spaces — pass through unchanged. Non-anchor angle-bracket sequences (e.g. `<b>`) are NOT collapsed; their brackets are encoded literally.

trigger_keywords: how is the start message composed
what does the start_message output look like
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The start line format is exactly `<project_name> - <escaped_build_name> ` — note the single trailing space after the build name. The project name is NOT escaped, only the build name goes through the escaper. Empty names are allowed and produce empty segments, so two empty inputs yield ` -  `.

The channel string is split on commas, semicolons, and spaces (any combination). One HTTP request is fired per named channel. An empty channel string still produces exactly one request to an empty target — it does NOT produce zero requests.

trigger_keywords: what URL is used in webhook mode
how is the webhook endpoint constructed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In webhook mode the URL is `https://<team_domain>.slack.com/services/hooks/jenkins-ci?token=<token>`. The same URL is used for every channel in a multi-channel fan-out — the channel name does not appear in the webhook URL.

trigger_keywords: how is the bot endpoint routed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: In bot mode the URL is `https://slack.com/api/chat.postMessage?token=<token>&channel=<channel_without_hash>&link_names=1&as_user=true`. The leading `#` is stripped from the channel name. If the channel string contains a colon like `channel:timestamp`, the part before the colon is the channel (hash stripped) and the part after is appended as `&thread_ts=<timestamp>`.

trigger_keywords: how is overall delivery success determined
what counts as a failed delivery
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Delivery is `true` only if every single HTTP request returned status 200. If any request returns non-200, the overall result is `false`. The `fail_alternate` flag makes the mock transport alternate success/failure across requests (first succeeds, second fails, third succeeds, etc.), which also makes the overall delivered result `false`. Transport failures must never propagate as exceptions — always surface as a boolean outcome.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid slot keys
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The valid slot keys are: `default`, `success`, `aborted`, `not_built`, `unstable`, `failure`. The `populated_slots` output lists only the non-empty slots in that exact canonical order (default first, failure last), as a comma-separated string. If no slots are populated, `populated_slots=` is printed with an empty value (not omitted).

trigger_keywords: can the core be used without JSON
is the core coupled to stdin
separation between adapter and core logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The JSON stdin/stdout adapter is strictly separated from core logic. All escaping, routing, delivery, and config checks must be callable as ordinary in-language function calls without any JSON or stdin dependency. The adapter is the ONLY component that reads stdin JSON and writes stdout. The transport (HTTP client) must be injectable so tests can use an in-memory recorder without making real HTTP calls.

trigger_keywords: how to run the test suite
output file naming convention for tests
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The test harness is invoked as `bash rcb_tests/test.sh` with an optional `--cases-dir <subdir>` argument (default `test_cases`). For each case it writes raw stdout to `rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/<filename_stem>@<zero_padded_3_digit_index>.txt` (e.g. `feature1_message_escaping@000.txt`). The file contains ONLY raw stdout — no PASS/FAIL metadata. Different `--cases-dir` values write to different subdirectories so they never overwrite each other.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid op field values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which op strings does the adapter support
what are the possible op inputs
tell me all the op names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'op' field. Valid values are: 'escape', 'start_message', 'publish', 'custom_message_populated'. Each value maps to one feature.

show me feature1 message escaping test cases
what are the feature1 message escaping test inputs
give me the feature1_message_escaping test file
what does feature1 message escaping expect

show me feature2 start message test cases

give me the feature2_start_message test file
what does feature2 start message expect

show me feature3 1 delivery fanout test cases
what are the feature3 1 delivery fanout test inputs

test cases for feature4 custom message populated
show me feature4 custom message populated test cases

context_pointers.vague_reference: similar to what we did in the old Jenkins integration, you can check that codebase for reference
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The escaping logic mirrors the Slack/Jenkins plugin conventions: specifically the `<URL|LABEL>` inline-link syntax used by Slack's message formatting dialect, and the three reserved characters `&`, `<`, `>` that must be entity-encoded per Slack's API documentation.

context_pointers.vague_reference: two different ways we send messages depending on the credential type
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The `mode` field in the publish command: `webhook` mode posts to `https://<team_domain>.slack.com/services/hooks/jenkins-ci?token=<token>`, while `bot` mode posts to `https://slack.com/api/chat.postMessage` with channel/link_names/as_user query parameters.

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect documented right now — could you check the existing Jenkins Slack plugin codebase or the Slack API docs for that behavior? If it's about message formatting or delivery routing, try looking at the test case JSON files under `rcb_tests/public_test_cases/` for the exact expected input/output contract.

feature1_message_escaping.json.description: Escape arbitrary notification text into the chat workspace's display dialect: HTML anchor tags become inline links, the angle brackets and ampersand that the chat protocol reserves are entity-encoded, and ordinary punctuation such as percent signs and curly braces pass through untouched. The command is {"op":"escape","text":<raw text>} and the program prints a single line `escaped=<rendered text>`.

feature2_start_message.json.description: Compose the opening line of a build notification from a project's display name and a build's display name. The line is rendered as `<project> - <build> ` (note the single trailing space), and the build name is passed through the same escaping rules as free text, so anchor tags collapse into inline links and reserved markup characters are entity-encoded. The command is {"op":"start_message","project_name":<name>,"build_name":<name>} and the program prints `message=<rendered line>`.

cases.expected_output: message=project - #43 Started by changes from Bob

feature3_1_delivery_fanout.json.description: Deliver a notification to one or more chat channels over HTTP and report transport-level outcomes. The target channel string may name several channels separated by commas/semicolons/spaces; the sender fires exactly one HTTP request per channel. Delivery is considered successful only when every request returns HTTP 200; any non-200 response (or an alternating-failure pattern across multiple channels) makes the overall delivery fail. The command is {"op":"publish","mode":"webhook","team_domain":<d>,"token":<t>,"channel":<c>,"status":<http status the transport returns>,"fail_alternate":<bool>} and the program prints `requests=<n>`, `delivered=<bool>`, and one `request_<i>_url=<url>` line per request.

feature3_2_endpoint_routing.json.description: Select the upstream endpoint and request shape from the sending mode. In webhook mode the message is posted to the team's incoming-webhook path derived from the team domain. In bot mode the message is posted to the chat post-message API with the channel, link-name expansion and as-user flags encoded as query parameters; the leading '#' is stripped. When the channel carries a thread timestamp after a colon (channel:timestamp), bot mode adds a thread parameter. The command is {"op":"publish","mode":"webhook"|"bot","team_domain":<d>,"token":<t>,"channel":<c>,"status":200} and the program prints `requests=1`, `delivered=true`, and `request_1_url=<routed url>`.

feature4_custom_message_populated.json.description: Report whether any custom-message slot has been filled. A notification config exposes several optional custom-message slots (a default one plus per-outcome overrides for success, aborted, not-built, unstable and failure). The check answers true as soon as at least one slot holds a non-empty string, and false when every provided slot is empty or absent. The command is {"op":"custom_message_populated","slots":{<slot>:<text>,...}} and the program prints `any_populated=<bool>` followed by `populated_slots=<comma-separated slot names that held non-empty text>`.
